title: Cash Buyers vs Estate Agents in Greenwich: An Honest Comparison
excerpt: A balanced look at selling your Greenwich property to a cash buyer versus using an estate agent, with real costs, timelines, and local market analysis.
metaDescription: Cash buyers vs estate agents in Greenwich: honest comparison of costs, timelines, and outcomes. Best way to sell property in the Royal Borough in 2025.
content:
Two Paths to Selling Your Greenwich Home

When the time comes to sell your property in the Royal Borough of Greenwich, you face a fundamental choice. You can list with an estate agent and sell on the open market, or you can sell directly to a cash property buyer for a guaranteed sum and a fixed completion date. Both routes have genuine advantages, and the right decision depends on your personal circumstances, timeline, and priorities.

This guide delivers an honest, balanced comparison using real-world figures from the Greenwich property market, helping you decide which path best serves your needs.

Selling Through an Estate Agent in Greenwich

The Process

You invite estate agents to value your property — perhaps a period conversion near the Cutty Sark, a family home in Eltham, or a flat in the Royal Arsenal development. After selecting an agent and agreeing fees, your property is marketed on Rightmove, Zoopla, and the agent's own channels. Viewings are arranged, offers negotiated, and a buyer found. That buyer then secures a mortgage, instructs a solicitor, commissions surveys, and — if everything holds together — the sale completes months later.

Realistic Costs in Greenwich

Let's use a concrete example: selling a three-bedroom Victorian terrace in Charlton with a market value of £480,000.

  • Estate agent fees: At 1.5% plus VAT, that's £8,640
  • Solicitor/conveyancer fees: £1,500-£2,500
  • EPC certificate: £80-£120
  • Potential repair costs post-survey: £1,000-£5,000
  • Ongoing costs during 5-month sale: Mortgage payments, council tax, insurance, utilities — potentially £10,000-£15,000

Total realistic costs: £21,220-£31,260. Your net proceeds from a £480,000 sale could be as low as £449,000-£459,000.

Timeline

In the current Greenwich market, expect: 2-4 weeks for marketing and generating offers, 1-2 weeks for negotiation, then 8-16 weeks for conveyancing. Total: approximately 3-6 months. If the first buyer falls through — affecting roughly 30% of sales nationally — add several more months.

Advantages

Open market exposure can generate competitive offers, particularly for desirable properties in areas like Blackheath and Greenwich Town. Multiple interested buyers can bid against each other, potentially driving the price above your expectations. For premium properties in prime locations, this competitive tension can be valuable.

Disadvantages

Uncertainty is the fundamental problem. Nothing is legally binding until contracts are exchanged, which typically happens just weeks before completion. Your buyer can withdraw at any point, for any reason, at no cost to themselves. Chain dependency multiplies the risk — your sale depends on multiple other transactions, each with its own potential points of failure.

Selling to a Cash Buyer in Greenwich

The Process

You contact a cash buying company, provide your property details, and receive a cash offer within 24 hours. If you accept, the buyer's solicitors handle conveyancing, a brief property assessment is arranged, and the sale completes on a date you choose — typically within 7-21 days.

Costs

Using the same Charlton property worth £480,000 on the open market:

  • Estate agent fees: £0
  • Solicitor fees: £0 (covered by the buyer)
  • EPC: £0
  • Repair costs: £0 (bought in any condition)
  • Ongoing costs: Negligible given the 7-21 day timeline

Total costs: £0. The offer price equals the amount you receive.

The Price Trade-Off

Cash buyers typically offer 75-85% of the open-market value. For our £480,000 Charlton terrace, that's approximately £360,000-£408,000. The headline difference from the full market price looks significant. But compare the cash offer of £408,000 (at 85%) with the net proceeds of £449,000-£459,000 from a traditional sale after costs, and the gap shrinks to £41,000-£51,000. For that difference, you're buying certainty, speed, and the elimination of months of stress and uncertainty.

Advantages

Speed — completion in days rather than months. Certainty — guaranteed sale with no chain risk. Zero costs — no fees of any kind. Flexibility — sell in any condition without repairs. Control — you choose the completion date. These advantages compound in value when you're dealing with time-sensitive situations or when you can't afford a sale to fail.

Disadvantages

The lower offer price is the primary trade-off. While the net difference is smaller than it appears, you will likely receive less than through a successful open-market sale. The emphasis is on 'successful' — the traditional route only delivers its full price advantage if everything goes right, which it doesn't in approximately 30% of cases.

When a Cash Buyer Is the Better Choice for Greenwich Homeowners

  • Time-sensitive situations: Relocations, financial deadlines, or impending repossession
  • Properties needing work: Inherited homes in Eltham, run-down rentals in Plumstead, or properties with structural issues
  • After a chain collapse: When you can't face the risk of another failed sale
  • Difficult-to-mortgage properties: Non-standard construction in Thamesmead, short leases, or properties with complex legal titles
  • Sensitive situations: Divorce, bereavement, or health issues where simplicity and certainty are paramount

When an Estate Agent Is the Better Choice

  • No time pressure: You can comfortably wait 6+ months for the best possible price
  • Premium property in prime condition: A beautifully presented home in Blackheath or Greenwich Town that will generate strong open-market interest
  • Maximum price is everything: You're willing to accept the risks, costs, and delays in pursuit of the highest possible figure
  • Strong market conditions: When buyer demand in your specific area is high and chain risks are lower

Protecting Yourself Either Way

If choosing an estate agent, verify their professional accreditation, understand their contract terms (particularly tie-in periods), and ensure their valuation is realistic. Be wary of agents who significantly overvalue your property to win your instruction — this leads to price reductions and extended marketing periods.

If choosing a cash buyer, verify their credentials: NAPB membership, independent reviews on Trustpilot and Google, and a clear written offer with no hidden conditions. Never pay upfront fees, and be cautious of any buyer who significantly increases their initial verbal offer just to secure your agreement — this is often a tactic followed by a later reduction.

A Third Option: Sell to a Cash Buyer, Then Buy Chain-Free

An increasingly popular strategy in Greenwich is to sell to a cash buyer first, bank the proceeds, and then approach your onward purchase as a chain-free buyer. This position gives you enormous bargaining power — sellers love chain-free purchasers because they represent certainty. You may find that the negotiating advantage on your purchase partially offsets the lower price received from the cash buyer, making the overall financial outcome surprisingly competitive.

Get Both Valuations — It Costs Nothing

We strongly recommend getting both a cash buyer offer and estate agent valuations before making your decision. Both are free and place you under no obligation. Compare the figures, factor in all costs and risks, and choose the path that best fits your needs.
